title: Garden water resilience during hosepipe bans
subtitle: What hosepipe ban restrictions cover, what is still permitted, and how to manage a garden through a dry spell
excerpt: Hosepipe ban orders restrict the use of a hosepipe to water a garden directly from a tap, but watering cans, drip irrigation systems on timers, and stored rainwater are typically still allowed. Planning for a ban in advance means having enough stored water, the right irrigation setup, and soil conditions that reduce how much water a garden needs. This explains the restrictions, permitted workarounds, and the most effective resilience measures.
sourceNotes: Water Industry Act 1991 Section 76 (hosepipe ban powers). Defra guidance on temporary use ban orders. Individual water company published ban conditions (Thames Water, Southern Water, South East Water - all public domain). Environment Agency drought guidance.

content.text: Hosepipe bans in England and Wales are temporary use ban (TUB) orders made by water companies under the Water Industry Act 1991. Each company publishes its specific restrictions, which vary in detail. The core prohibition is using a hosepipe connected to a mains tap to water a private garden or washing a private car. The prohibited activity is the hosepipe connected to the tap, not the act of watering itself.
What is typically still permitted during a ban
Watering with a watering can filled from a tap.
Drip irrigation systems on automated timers. Check your water company's order: some bans exempt drip irrigation fed from a water butt but not from the mains.
Using stored rainwater from a butt or tank, including with a hosepipe attached to the butt (not to a mains tap).
Watering newly planted trees, shrubs, or turf laid within the previous 28 days is sometimes exempted; verify with the specific order.

Check your water company's order directly. The exact terms vary. Thames Water, Southern Water, and South East Water publish full TUB conditions on their websites and issue updates as restrictions change during a drought event.
Preparing before a ban is declared

Install a water butt: a 200-litre butt linked to a downpipe fills after 3-4 mm of rain on a standard house roof. During May and June, before drought conditions peak, the butt fills regularly. One butt covers watering can use for a small garden; two linked butts give 3-4 weeks of reserve for a medium plot.
Mulch all beds to 75-100 mm depth in spring. Mulch reduces evaporation from the soil surface by 50-70%, significantly reducing the amount of water a garden needs during dry spells.
Install drip irrigation on vegetable beds. A gravity-fed drip system from a raised water butt operates without mains pressure and without a hosepipe connected to a tap.

Improve soil water retention by adding compost annually. Sandy soils lose water rapidly; a 20 mm compost mulch worked in each year significantly increases the soil's water-holding capacity over 3-5 seasons.
During a ban: prioritising water use
Water at the base of plants, not the leaves. Deep, infrequent watering encourages deep root development; shallow, frequent watering does not.
Water in the evening (or early morning). Evening watering loses less to evaporation than daytime application and gives roots time to absorb before the next day's heat.

Priority order: fruiting vegetables (tomatoes, courgettes, beans) first, then leaf crops, then ornamentals, then lawn. Lawns recover from drought on their own. Established shrubs and trees can also go several weeks without extra water.

attrs.symptom: Water butt runs dry within the first week of a ban
attrs.cause: Storage capacity is insufficient for garden demand, or garden area is too large relative to catchment
attrs.fix: Add a second butt linked in series. Reduce watered area during the ban to highest-priority plants only. Collect household washing-up water in a basin for watering can use.

attrs.symptom: Not sure whether a particular watering method is permitted
attrs.cause: Ban conditions vary between water companies and are sometimes updated during an event
attrs.fix: Check the water company website directly. If the FAQ does not cover the specific method, call the customer line. Water companies are required to publish the full TUB conditions.
